text: Highest and Best Use criteria: The reasonably probable and legal use of vacant land or an improved property that is physically possible, appropriately supported, financially feasible, and that results in the highest value. The four criteria the highest and best use must meet are legal permissibility, physical possibility, financial feasibility, and maximum productivity.

Please explain the discrepancy  The Great Room's label on the sketch has been updated to reflect its label on the picture page.

Extra Comments ANSI Standards Gross living area calculations are derived from the measurement of the subject property and reported on the dimension and sketch pages of this report. Depreciation is estimated by the appraiser observations. The appraiser has adhered to ANSI standards in measuring the subject property.  Clarification of Intended Use and Intended User: First State Mortgage The Intended User of this appraisal report is the Lender/Client. The Intended Use is to evaluate the property that is the subject of this appraisal for a mortgage finance transaction, subject to the stated Scope of Work, purpose of the appraisal, reporting requirements of this appraisal report form, and Definition of Market Value. No additional Intended Users are identified by the appraiser.  Effective January 1st, 2010, the ETHICS RULE now requires an appraiser to disclose any prior services provided regarding the subject property within the three years preceding the new request / assignment. The appraiser has not appraised the property in the past three years preceeding this request.   This appraisal report adheres to the development and reporting options of The Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP), FIRREA Title XI Appraisal Regulations and The Appraisal Independence Standards set forth by H.R 4173.  Geographical Competency Statement: This appraiser has geographic competency. The appraiser has prepared the appraisal in a familiar location and has spent sufficient time to understand the nuances of the local market and the supply and demand factors relating to the specific property type and the location involved.  Section 1455.245  Scope of Property Condition Inspections by Real Estate Appraisers   Licensed real estate appraisers may include in an appraisal report comments on the condition of the property that affect property value, including physical deficiencies, adverse conditions and renovations, based on observations by the licensed real estate appraiser during the appraisal of the property. Licensed real estate appraisers may not provide these comments for compensation, or with the expectation of receiving compensation, directly or indirectly, as a substitute for a home inspection, or a home inspection report, prepared by a licensed home inspector. All appraisal reports of a property that is 1 to 4 residential units prepared by licensed real estate appraisers must include the following statement: "The comments by the licensed real estate appraiser contained within this appraisal report on the condition of the property do not address "standards of practice" as defined in the Home Inspector License Act [225 ILCS 441] and 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1410 and are not to be considered a home inspection or home inspection report."  ILLINOIS FEE DISCLOSURE: To comply with Illinois state regulations, when an AMC engages an appraiser for an assignment, the appraiser must prominently display the appraisal fee received from the AMC in the certification as follows:  The compensation for this appraisal assignment is $450.00

Underwriter Conditions  The following in reference to comparable #3 to be addressed by appraiser:  The appraiser has reported materially different GLA(s) in one or more appraisal reports. Verify that the GLA is accurate.  - The public record and MLS listing of Comparable #3 has been reexamined and confirms the reported GLA of 1322 sq.ft.  The appraiser has reported materially different above-grade bedroom count(s) in one or more appraisal reports. Verify that the bedroom count is accurate.  - The public record and MLS listing of Comparable #3 has been reexamined and confirms the reported bedroom count of 3.   The appraiser has reported materially different basement area(s) in one or more appraisal reports. Verify that the basement area is accurate.  - The public record and MLS listing of Comparable #3 differs in reported unfinished basement square footage. The MLS listing details of a partial basement was deemed the most accurate information made avaliable to the appraiser and was used in this report.   The appraiser has reported materially different age(s) in one or more appraisal reports. Verify that the age is accurate.  - The public record and MLS listing of Comparable #3 differs in the property's reported age. The MLS listing reports the property was built in 1926 and  was deemed as the most accurate based on the information made avaliable to the appraiser.  The appraiser has reported a materially different condition in one or more appraisal reports. Verify that the condition rating is accurate and consistent with the UAD definition.  - The avaliable photos of Comparable #3 have been reexamined and the condition rating contained in this report is consistent with the UAD definition of Condition 3 rating.  - Every appraisal report completed by the appraiser between 04/01/2024 and 10/07/2024 have been analyzed and the use of Comparable #3 has not been identified in previous reports. Can you please provide evidence of such incidents to correct my previous appraisal and/or improve my current appraisal records?

Underwriter Conditions  The reported age of comparable #3 indicates that the condition should likely be a "C1" or "C2". Appraiser to confirm that the condition rating and age are accurate per the UAD Appendix D definition of "C1" and "C2".  - Comparable #3's condition and adjustment hav been updated to reflect the UAD Appendix D definition of "C2". These changes, however, do not impact the estimate of market value.  The appraised value is below the range of unadjusted comparable sales prices provided by the appraiser. Appraiser to address that there is supporting evidence for the value conclusion.  - The subject was sold below market value for the neighborhood. It was confirmed by the listing agent that this was intentionally done by the seller to sell the property quickly. The estimate of value not being bracketed by a comparable sale within close proximity to the subject is accounted for by the lack of recent comparable sales in the market area and the difference in condition between the subject and exisitng comparable sales.    The quality adjustment for both comparable #2 & #3 is smaller than peer and model adjustments. Appraiser to please address.  - The quality adjustments for Comparable #2 & #3 reflect the difference in exterior ornamentation when compared to the subject property. The quality adjustments applied to Comparable #2 & #3 are sufficient to demonstrate these improvements.

Appraiser to confirm the following for property's multiple parcels: 1) Each parcel must be conveyed in its entirety,  - Each parcel has been conveyed in its entirety.   2) Parcels must be adjoined to the other, unless they comply with the following exception. Parcels that otherwise would be adjoined, but are divided by a road, are acceptable if the parcel without a residence is a non-buildable lot (for example, waterfront properties where the parcel without the residence provides access to the water).  -The two parcels are adjoined as shown on the plat map.   3) Each parcel must have the same basic zoning (for example, residential, agricultural).  - The two parcels have the same basic zoning.   4) The entire property may contain only one dwelling unit.  -The entire property only contains one dwelling unit.   5) Confirmation the additional parcel is not buildable.  -It appears that the additional adjoined parcel is buildable.   2. Appraiser to add $1710 seller concessions per contract.  - The report has been updated to reflect the seller concessions listed in the contract.   3. Appraiser to confirm if there are any health or safety concerns with the four sheds on the subject property that hold significant physical depreciation and have no remaining economic life.  - Although the four sheds on the subject property hold significant physical depreciation, a visual inspection of each shed did not indicate any immediate health or safety concerns to be addressed. The structures appeared to be stable, but due to their age and condition they have diminished utilitiy.   4. Appraiser to address SSR red flags: 1) Comp #3- The age varies from public records. Report= 102 yrs, public records= 52 yrs). 2) The subject property GLA (1450 sf) varies by more than 10% from public records (900 sf) please confirm GLA is accurate. 3) The view type noted for all comparable sales differ from the subject property's view type. Please ensure the comparables provided are the most appropriate.  1. The sketch included on the public record's property card indicates that the dwelling of Comparable #3 was constructed in 1923.   2. The public record property card of the subject property appears to not take into account the finished second floor GLA. The appraiser has adhered to ANSI standards in measuring the subject property.  3. A wide search was made to identify recent comparable sales with a similar view as the subject, but none were found. The comparables used in this report were the most applicable recent sales in the market area.   Mortgage insurance company requesting appraiser to provide a comparable sale with similar view/location - commercial RR/Busy Rd. if none available please address marketability of the location to RR /Commercial/Busy Rd.  -" An extensive search was made to find a recent comparable sale with a similar view as the subject. Comparable #6 was included to demonstrate the marketability of houses of a similar age, condition, quality, and view of wooded land, agricultural land, commercial buildings, and rail road tracks. It has been adjusted for its site size, GLA, garage space count, enclosed porch, and shed. " This statement has been added to the commentary section below the sales comparison grid. The inclusion of Comparable #6 has further supported the estimate of market value and demostrated the marketability of houses with a similar view as the subject.   An extensive search was made to find a recent comparable sale with a similar view of a busy road as the subject, however, none were identified. The subject's acreage and ample frontage between the dwelling and the busy road mitigates potential adverse impact on its marketability.    please correct parcel Id - should be 07-07-29-425-001 (not 452)  - The parcel ID has been updated to reflect 07-07-29-425-001. I apologize for the inconvenience.

This appraisal report adheres to the development and reporting options of The Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP), FIRREA Title XI Appraisal Regulations and The Appraisal Independence Standards set forth by H.R 4173.  Geographical Competency Statement: This appraiser has geographic competency. The appraiser has prepared the appraisal in a familiar location and has spent sufficient time to understand the nuances of the local market and the supply and demand factors relating to the specific property type and the location involved.  Section 1455.245  Scope of Property Condition Inspections by Real Estate Appraisers   Licensed real estate appraisers may include in an appraisal report comments on the condition of the property that affect property value, including physical deficiencies, adverse conditions and renovations, based on observations by the licensed real estate appraiser during the appraisal of the property. Licensed real estate appraisers may not provide these comments for compensation, or with the expectation of receiving compensation, directly or indirectly, as a substitute for a home inspection, or a home inspection report, prepared by a licensed home inspector. All appraisal reports of a property that is 1 to 4 residential units prepared by licensed real estate appraisers must include the following statement: "The comments by the licensed real estate appraiser contained within this appraisal report on the condition of the property do not address "standards of practice" as defined in the Home Inspector License Act [225 ILCS 441] and 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1410 and are not to be considered a home inspection or home inspection report."
